raw.notes: Mufaddal Saifuddin current Da'i; Tayyibi Ismaili

raw.narrative_en: The 'Imād Shāhīs ruled briefly in Berar as the smallest of the Deccan sultanates. They were annexed by the Nizām Shāhīs.

The 'Ādil Shāhīs were a dynasty that established one of the Deccan's most powerful sultanates centered on Bijapur, building the Gol Gumbaz. The reign of Ibrāhīm 'Ādil Shāh II represents the pinnacle of cultural and artistic patronage. They ruled for approximately 200 years until Mughal Awrangzīb's conquests.
The Barīd Shāhīs ruled centered on Bidar as one of the Bahmanid successor sultanates. They ended with annexation by the 'Ādil Shāhīs.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Nawwāb-Viziers and Kings of Oudh (Awadh)
raw.narrative_en: The Nawwābs of Awadh were a dynasty that created a wealthy Islamic cultural center with Shī'ī character centered on Lucknow. Lucknow's literary, musical, and architectural tradition took shape in this period. The British annexation in 1856 ignited one of the most violent fronts of the 1857 Great Rebellion a year later.

The Nizām Shāhīs were a Bahmanid successor sultanate centered on Ahmednagar, entering history through Chand Bībī's heroic resistance against the Mughals. Ruling for approximately 145 years, they were conquered by Mughal Shāhjahān.

The Nizāms of Hyderabad were a dynasty that established India's largest and wealthiest princely state by gaining independence from Mughal Deccan governorship. Maintaining independence until 1948, Hyderabad survived as a British ally and its incorporation into India was only achieved through 'Police Action.'

The Qutb Shāhīs were a dynasty of Turkmen origin that established one of the wealthiest Deccan sultanates centered on Golconda. Golconda's diamond mines — including the Koh-i Noor — produced world-famous treasures. Founding the city of Hyderabad and building the Charminar, the Qutb Shāhīs were the architects of the Telugu-Islamic cultural synthesis.
The Sharqī Sultans of Jawnpur were a small dynasty that separated from the Delhi Sultanate, ruling briefly and notable for their architectural legacy.

The Sultans of Gujarat established a powerful Islamic state centered on Ahmedabad on India's western coast, shaped by maritime trade and Portuguese rivalry. Ahmedabad's architectural legacy forms the most lasting trace of this era.

raw.parent_tribe: Şâh Mîr Svâtî ailesi; Çek ailesi
raw.narrative_en: The Sultans of Kashmir were a dynasty forming a distinctive branch of Islamic civilization in the sheltered Himalayan valley. Sultan Zayn al-'Ābidīn created Kashmir's golden age through his tolerance policy between Hindus and Muslims. The Kashmir shawl and handicraft traditions took root in this period.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Abdālī or Durrānī Rulers and Kings of Afghanistan
raw.regions_all: Kandahar; Kâbil; Herât; Peşâver; Keşmir (geçici); Pencap (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Durrānī and Bārakzay rulers represent modern Afghanistan, founded by Aḥmad Shāh Durrānī. While Aḥmad Shāh is regarded as the 'father of Afghanistan,' the Bārakzay period was shaped by three Anglo-Afghan Wars. Afghanistan's buffer state identity formed during this era.

raw.regions_all: Gûr (Fîrûzkûh); Gazne; Lahor; Delhi; Bâmiyân; Tohâristân; Bedehşân
raw.narrative_en: The Ghūrids were an Iranian dynasty emerging from Afghanistan's mountainous Ghur region, ensuring Islam's permanent entry into India. Mu'izz al-Dīn's victory at the Battle of Tarain (1192) opened northern India to the Islamic world, while they left masterpieces like the Minaret of Jam.

raw.regions_all: Delhi; Agra; Lahor; Dekkan; Bengal; Keşmir; Kâbil
raw.narrative_en: The Mughals built the most magnificent period of Islamic civilization in the Indian subcontinent on the Timurid legacy, establishing one of world history's wealthiest empires. Beginning with Babur's victory at Panipat (1526), the dynasty institutionalized the Hindu-Muslim synthesis through Akbar's unique tolerance policy (Sulh-i Kul). The Shah Jahan era reached the pinnacle of Islamic architecture with the construction of the Taj Mahal, while Aurangzeb extended the empire to its widest borders but accelerated centrifugal forces. Mughal court culture — miniature painting, garden tradition, the birth of Urdu — permanently shaped South Asia's cultural identity. The dynasty, reduced to a shadow empire by the increasing intervention of the British East India Company, ended with the exile of the last emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar after the 1857 Rebellion.

The Ghaznavids were a Turkic dynasty that gained independence from Samanid ghulam governorship, establishing a powerful empire from Afghanistan to northern India. Sultan Mahmud launched seventeen campaigns into India, initiating the first great wave of Islam's spread in the subcontinent. Patronizing Ferdowsi's Shahnameh and hosting universal scholars like Bīrūnī, Mahmud is one of Islamic history's most contradictory rulers.

raw.narrative_en: The Bahmanids were the dynasty that laid the foundations of Islamic culture in the Deccan (southern India) and ancestor of five successor sultanates. Their architectural legacy in Gulbarga and Bidar is among the most original examples of Indian Islamic art.

raw.narrative_en: The Delhi Sultanate was the state that laid the foundations of Islamic civilization in northern India under six different dynasties over three centuries, permanently changing the subcontinent's political-cultural map. As the Ghulam (Mamluk), Khalji, Tughluq, Sayyid, and Lodi dynasties succeeded each other, Delhi became one of the world's largest cities. The magnificent court visited by Ibn Battuta, the successful repulsion of Mongol invasions during the Khalji period, and Tughluq Muhammad bin Tughluq's contradictory but visionary reforms are prominent pages of this era. Delhi's architectural legacy — from the Qutb Minar to Tughluqabad — reflects the original interpretation of Islamic art in India.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Governors and Sultans of Bengal
raw.parent_tribe: Çeşitli: Delhi valileri, İlyâs Şâhîler, Habeşîler, Seyyidler, Sûrîler
raw.narrative_en: The Bengal Sultans and Governors were dynasties that ruled Bengal for 370 years in nine different branches after gaining independence from northern India, governing one of South Asia's wealthiest regions. The Abyssinian (Ethiopian) period of rule presents a rare example of ethnic diversity in Islamic history, while the Husayn Shāh era represents the pinnacle of Bengali Islamic culture. They ended with Mughal Akbar's annexation.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Nawwāb-Viziers and Nawwāb-Nāzims of Bengal
raw.narrative_en: The Nawwāb-Viziers of Bengal were a dynasty that became effectively independent from Mughal Bengal governorship, ruling the wealthy Bengal delta. The 1757 Battle of Plassey constitutes one of the turning points of world history as the starting point of the British East India Company's colonial domination in India.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Sultans and Rulers of Mālwa
raw.narrative_en: The Sultans of Malwa were a dynasty ruling for 170 years in central India centered on the Mandu fortress. Governing in three branches, the Malwa Sultanate maintained its independence by utilizing Mandu's natural fortress geography.

raw.regions_all: Semerkand; Herât; İsfahan; Şîraz; Bağdat (geçici); Hindistan (geçici); Anadolu (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Timurids were the dynasty founded by Timur from the Barlas Turco-Mongol clan, transforming Samarkand into a world capital. Timur fundamentally redrew the political map of the Islamic world through military campaigns stretching from China to India, from Russian steppes to the Mediterranean. Yet the true Timurid legacy lies in cultural rather than military achievements: Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory broke new ground in astronomy, while the Herat miniature school represents the pinnacle of Islamic art. The era of Husayn Bayqara and Alī Shīr Navā'ī is the golden age of Chagatai Turkish literature. The Timurid cultural legacy was carried to India through the Mughals and to Iran through the Safavids.

Mysore's Muslim rulers Hyder Ali and his son Tipu Sultan led the last great resistance against British colonialism in southern India. Tipu Sultan, nicknamed the 'Tiger of Mysore,' was a pioneer in military use of rocket weapons, dying in battle during the 1799 Siege of Seringapatam.

raw.regions_all: Meşhed; İsfahan; Delhi (geçici sefer); Buhara (geçici sefer)
raw.narrative_en: The Afshārids were a short-lived Iranian dynasty founded through Nādir Shāh's extraordinary military genius, entering history with the enormous booty of the Delhi campaign (Koh-i Noor, Peacock Throne). The empire rapidly disintegrated after Nādir Shāh's assassination.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Sultans of Ma'bar or Madura
raw.narrative_en: The Sultans of Ma'bar represented a short-lived Islamic rule in southern India centered on Madura. Established when Delhi Tughluq governors gained independence, the sultanate was destroyed by the Vijayanagara Hindu Empire.

raw.parent_tribe: 1. Argun boyu (Moğol/Türk); 2. Muhammed Îsâ Tarhân kolu
raw.narrative_en: The Arghūns and Tarkhāns were a Turco-Mongol dynasty ruling in two branches centered on Thatta in Sind. They ended with Mughal Akbar's annexation of Sind.
